speaker: interviewer
text: The interview with Ellis Lewin, tape number four. Mr Lewin, we left off with your talking about boarding the train, leaving the Lodz ghetto. Would you like to continue and tell us about the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train, as I said earlier, it was just packed with, with, with, human beings, and, and-- virtually on top of each other. And once we were in there, they shut the doors on us. And hysteria just broke loose. And it was just like being trapped and having nowhere to go. And I can just tell you that, again, I'm talking from the perspective of a child. The horror and the feeling was unimaginable to me to this day. And people were just unable to breathe. They were just unable-- they we were just, just, packed in on top of each other. I believe the journey took-- I thought it was forever-- but I guess from the Lodz to Auschwitz was about two days. I thought it was earlier, or less time, but I believe it was like two days or more, maybe three days. And people were virtually defecating and relieving themselves on each other. And it was just-- it was just something beyond description. And the sound of the, of the, of the tracks and the wheels and the whistle of the train are the things that are most memorable to me. I don't know where we were going. All we kept doing was just embracing each other And silently just mourning--

speaker: interviewer
text: Each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moaning and crying. My mother, my sister, and my dad. And everyone else who still had their families were just in a silent-- after the very first few hours of realizing what was going on, it was just like some hysteria. But, basically, you sort of settle into a, a unimaginable silence and helplessness. And you just deal with it to the next level. And-- --As bad as you think things are, they're just the beginning of what was to happen. When we arrived to Auschwitz--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do the trains stop at all when you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to my recollection. Because it was loaded up, and there was-- not to my recollection, it was-- it didn't. We got in in the morning, into-- some time during the morning, because it was daylight. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just-- you couldn't describe it in any way if you wanted to do it in a horror movie, or in a novel, or whatever. The minute they opened the wagons, it was just total, complete misery. Beatings, and screamings, and beatings, and barking of dogs, and raw growling of dogs, and whistles of trains, and screaming, and beating, and screaming. And commands given. It was just-- it was just like, you open the doors, and all of a sudden, you find yourself in this inferno, in this, in this, in this unimaginable horror that you, as a adult or a child, would see in nightmares. And it was just coming through. And we were just holding on to each other. And I don't know, within minutes, my mother and my sister were dragged to one side. And I was dragged with my dad to another. We were told to go to another side. And I never had a chance to say goodbye to my mother, never had a chance to say goodbye to my sister. The, the, the, the pace, the speed of this, this thing, it was done by design. It was done to both-- for the person not to be able to comprehend, or understand, or in any way be able to think for a second what was happening. It was just incredible. It was just an incredible situation. And it was just-- as I tell it to you now, it's difficult for me to even describe it, because it was happening on a, on a minute-to-minute situation. And I got into the-- i got this line. It was this big line. And I saw my mother on the other side. There was another side, where the women went. And I, I never saw her cry. I don't-- I never saw her reach out. She just-- the last time I saw, she was hanging on to my sister. And my dad hollered to her, you hang-- you take care of her. I'll take care of him, in Yiddish. And whatever we have to do. This was the last word I heard. And my dad threw me in front of him. And he says, keep walking very tall. Don't even-- because we were observing what was going on in the front-- you know, in the front of the lines. And the very-- the one thing you didn't want is for the Germans to see that you were holding on to your child, because that was the whole idea, is to break up the family, murder the family. That was the genocide of the whole thing. So by not identifying that this is your child, there was a little bit of an edge you had to possibly survive, the fact that you are on your own and you sort of didn't belong to any family. By some miracle-- and, again, I tell you that this is, this was so fast and so incredibly-- within minutes I see these Germans. And there was all commands. I just-- I couldn't understand any of it. [GERMAN], you know. Today, of course, I know I learned to speak German because I had to. And I made it. I made it through the first phase of the inspection. It was-- how I made it, I have absolutely no idea. I think they were preoccupied. And those last transports or those last days, I'm not-- I'm hoping-- possibly second guessing, although I was part of this siege at that time, it seems like there was a lot of trains that are coming. Just was a lot of people. I don't know if that was a normal thing, but it was just a lot of transporting, and a lot of inspection. And that first phase of the inspection, they only took out young people, infants-- I mean, old people-- infants, people that could not walk, people that showed visibly defects, and were handicapped visibly. So, so the other ones-- and maybe they knew that they were going to get you on the next phase, because the next phase was in the, in the showers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that first inspection was, basically, not as, not as stringent as it would continue. So I made the first inspection, and my dad made it. And some of my uncles made it. In fact, almost everyone made it. In terms-- other ones, that weren't-- small infants and children to the, to the fact. And again, repeating, sick people and, and old people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the next phase we were in was-- I'm in this building. It was like a, like a sort of a warehouse building, and that's where they told us to remove our clothes. And, and there's another inspection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, at that inspection, I, I-- again, to me as a child, I was not looking and thinking in terms, will I survive, won't I survive? It's just that you keep moving. You just don't think about anything. You just try to protect that you're not going to get beat up, or slapped, or beaten.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, sure enough, I get thrown to the, to the bad side, in the, in this warehouse, in that inspection point. My dad is on the other side. And in there were kapos, Ukraine kapos, and-- [SIGHING] --They were unbelievable, just unbelievable cruel. So with that, my dad saw that. He runs up to a kapo-- and this sounds like some science fiction-- and when we were evacuating, or when we were leaving, when the Jews were leaving, or any people were leaving a place, they, they, they were very creative in hiding, since your-- your worldly possessions in those days was not dollars. It was not script money.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- your wealth was always in terms of diamonds or gold or-- because this is how you accumulated some sort of a-- what's the word I'm looking for? Assets. Whatever you want to call it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't measure them by money in the bank, because those banks were never part of, of any security. So people in those days just were trading on diamonds and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So my dad ran up to the kapo, and he says, here-- he had them hidden in his shoulders-- in the-- in the, in the shoulders in this [INAUDIBLE]. And he gave them a fairly good sized diamond.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, this is my son over there. He didn't have to negotiate with them. All he had do is take this diamond from him anyway, but he told him, he'd tell them where there's more, if he let me go on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, he told me to just stay there. He came over to me and told me just to stay. And I was-- there's a pile of clothes-- there was all these clothes that was taken off. And he told me just to stay there until this one part of-- this one section of these people in this warehouse, which was us, would leave. Then he would let me go to the other side. That's what he's telling my dad. And in that place, there were maybe 30% that didn't make it. OK? They went right to the gas chamber, which was adjacent to this warehouse. The showers were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew it. I saw it. I didn't see them dying, but I saw the-- it was like, ceramic-- white ceramic. And you went right from that place into that place, or you continued on to go into, into the main camp itself. And I stayed there. And for a few minutes, there was nobody left. And then he came in--

speaker: interviewer
text: He?

speaker: interviewee
text: This kapo came in. And my dad gave him everything else. It was-- you-- that's the way everybody hid their stuff. They hid them in their shoes. They hid them in their mouths. They hid them in their-- whatever it was. And he kept his word. For some unknown-- for some miracle, or whatever. I don't think he needed to. He could have taken it, and say-- but he kept his word for some unknown reason. It was a miracle. And I went in and I got my burlap uniform with the stripes, and I continued into the main camp, into Auschwitz itself, which was the main camp. And there, placed in a barrack with people that were already there prior to our arrival. And I became part of the, the resident of this particular barrack, which was another horror story of its own. There were bunks four high that you, that you had some straw, little straw that they gave you. The barrack consisted of like, horse windows. It was a, a wooden floor. And one end of the barrack was like, a 55-gallon drum, a cut 55-gallon drum. And this is where you had to go to relieve yourself, if you had to go. Well, needless to tell you, that dysentery, sickness, disease, which we-- was already there prior to our coming. We were comparatively healthy. So if you were a healthy person coming in there, other than hungry, you are, you are thrown into a, a infested, disease infested, condition. That doesn't take very long to succumb to that. And the, the thing that I'll never forget to this day is when I, I, I was given some of the food or some of the-- it was water soup and-- with some potato peelings. And I contracted dysentery, diarrhea. And I needed to make it to the, to this drum. That's the only way you can go is somehow find a position so you can sit on this drum, so you can go, so you can relieve yourself. And while you were going there, from one end to the barrack to the other, most of the people couldn't make it to this place. So they obviously just relieved themselves on this way, on this wooden plank, on this wooden floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were virtually stepping into people's waste. And you were squashing into this. And never made it to it because it was always running over. It was just-- it was just an incredible, horrible scene.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when you were trying to get back to your, to your bunk, to your place, you were covered with all this, and you had nothing to wipe yourself with. I mean, nothing. See, in Auschwitz, you were allowed to go to the bathroom in unison, and, and, and-- a certain time. Not when you needed to go. It's when they delegated for you to go. And it was like, during the day after like, maybe a meal or something. But sickness goes 24 hours. And your body and your system doesn't know from time. So we lost-- every day we lost hundreds and hundreds of people would just rotate out of these barracks. And that is, that is the most, to me, that is the most terrible time that I can remember it being in Auschwitz. I mean, I smelled the flesh. I mean, that was not, not the thing, although I didn't know it was flesh at the time. But the crematoriums were right, like, if I was-- my barrack was here, crematorium would be at the end of this driveway, which is maybe 150 feet. And the smoke, and the flesh, this burning of the flesh, was just common. It was like a daily occurrence, around the clock. Death was just everywhere, everywhere. You, you were no longer with the Germans themselves. You were now under the guidance of kapos, of, of, of kapos that were brought in from the Ukraine. Mostly, I remember were Ukraines. I'm not trying to put a tag on Ukraines as a whole, but for some unknown reason, the Germans-- well, they volunteered or-- how-- in whatever capacity, they stood out to me in Auschwitz as the culprits, or at least, they were in the ovens with the Jewish assistants-- with the-- they had the Jews that they delegated also, or they assigned, or they made you go-- to carry the bodies and so on. We had body details. They had all kinds of details there to, to accommodate the, the final solution, so to speak. Auschwitz was, to me, a horrendous place, because I saw my dad getting beat, beaten up, to the point where he just could not-- in fact, we were standing in an appel. And he was trying to, somehow, come over to my side. And for some unknown reason, they got on him, and they just started beating him unmercifully. And I tried--

speaker: interviewer
text: They?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the-- at that point, it was Germans and kapos both. And I tried to run out, and they give-- they hit me a couple of times. And my uncles, you know, grabbed me and, and, and just held me back. I mean, I was just doing it from the reaction of a child seeing his father getting beaten up to the point where he was just bleeding from every which way. And then they were pouring cold water on him. And that causes a heart-- I didn't know that-- I mean, but that was another method for them to kill you, is that they beat you to a point where your system gets so overheated, or your blood, or whatever it is, and then you pull water on somebody. I guess it causes some shock or whatever. I learned that later on. And so then, I had to cope with that, because we brought him back to the barracks, and he was just in agony. He just could not-- and he just kept-- he just could not move in or out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he had to drag himself out for the appels, and his ribs were broken. And he was just-- he was just dying, you know? And along with everything else that was happening, my father was suffering, you know, under, under these conditions. So Auschwitz was not a, a, a-- it was never a good place to begin with-- but that, that was my introduction to the true world of what I call the-- away from the ghetto, which now I, as you call it, I compared to as paradise, to what was going-- to what was happening to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was, again, hungry. We were, we were fed very, very little. We were just given like, a piece of dark bread in the morning. There was a water soup with the potato peelings for lunch. And, again, in the evening, it was like a potato-- there was water and a potato peeling in, in, in the evening. And that comprised the day of-- the full day of food. And we just hungry, just hungry, and, and, just, and just beaten down. And just-- there, I was with people from every country. We were with Hungarians, with Poles, with, with some from Czechoslovakia. There was a conglomeration of a lot of, a lot of Jews that were brought to Auschwitz and put together, but-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was to me, the worst camp. That was to me the worst camp on the conditions of being in a, what we call an exterminating camp, because that's, that, that was the only function that camp had. Was not to keep you alive, or at least, if you survive it, then you survive it, and you go on, to the next phase, which I will tell you about. But if you made it in Auschwitz, you're, you're kind of going to make it, unless there are other circumstances as you go on with, with this, with this journey. But Auschwitz, to me, was the absolute nightmare.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was Auschwitz about four to five weeks. And, and you know, I always tell people, I always tell friends, people, I've given some-- I was invited to make some-- to talk about my experiences in some schools and so on. And I said, you know, I said, if there's only one way I can tell you what I lived through, or someone like myself, a prisoner of war in Vietnam, or prisoners, people who, who have had some similar experience, is if I could somehow take you on a tour for one minute in Auschwitz, just for one minute. And you would hear the screams, the smell, the stench of, of, of of, human flesh, the exhibition of human beings to other human beings, and the exhibition of human beings dying under conditions that are from, are from one degree to another, from, from, any, any way and level of-- then that's all you would need to understand what the war, and what genocide, and what extermination is all about. Just one minute. If there was just one a minute-- and I'm sure that the, the, the captive soldier in Viet Nam and the conditions of the way they were treated, if they could see-- if I could just take one moment to that camp, and the way it was, it would explain everything. Unfortunately we can't do that. And fortunately, thank God, we don't have to. But that's what Auschwitz was to me. It is my worst nightmare, since I lost my mother, since I lost my sister, since that was my introduction to being alone. When I say alone, alone doesn't necessarily, you know, mean that are with anybody, that you're alone. You are alone, whether you're out there with your best friend, or your father, or whatever. Once you reach a place like that, you are on your own. You have to live by instinct, by some kind of a special whatever it is, because there's no one that can help you, other than yourself. And that's what Auschwitz represented to me. I became a survivor. I became a professional, the beginning of a professional survivor in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think helped you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe, with all my heart, that young people survived, because they didn't know any better. I believe that, as a child, you are blessed, that you don't know any better. The only thing that hurts you is the yearning of your mother, the memories of that good, wonderful feeling of your family. And the only thing that hurt you is if you get beat and you feel the pain, and when you're hungry, because you have no other way of thinking ahead what's, what's happening to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: The intellect, the educated people, the culture, the aristocracy of Europe, of any nation, of [? urban, ?] they are the ones that could not survive. They are the ones that went first, because they saw the predicament. They saw what was going on, and said, what is there to live for? I've lost everything, my family, my children, my, my life, my profession. So what if I do get liberated, if ever, if there ever will be such a thing? They let themselves go right away. They saw the [NON-ENGLISH]. As we say it. The, the, the, the true situation, the condition.

speaker: interviewee
text: A child was-- did not have that ability. So consequently, the children that survived, that were able to be, physically able to survive, did it only because we didn't know any better. And anyone that tells me anything else, I would have a problem with. So that's the answer to how I think I survived. When, of course, luck has a lot to do with. Luck has a lot to do in everything. But with it is a certain thing that you, you acquire, you become. It's just like, I imagine getting dropped in the jungle, and you have to now start to survive. And that's all I was in, was in the jungle, and I had to learn how to cope with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop now, as we come to the end of this tape. We'll continue in a minute.
